---
title: "Uncertainty in<br>Machine Learning"
author:
  - name: "Oliver Hennhöfer"
    affiliation: "Content"
  - name: "Qiongdan Shang"
    affiliation: "Design"
date: "March 29, 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, styles/custom.scss]
    navigation-mode: linear
    controls: true
    slide-number: false
    center: false
    include-after-body: scripts/smoke-include.html
---

## Scenario
You are driving to visit a friend in another city.
They ask when you will arrive so they can time dinner perfectly.<br>
You want to give them an accurate estimate.

## First Guess
You know the distance is about 200 km.<br>
From experience, this usually takes around 2 hours.

## Adding Information
You open a navigation app. It improves your estimate by using:

- Time of day
- Current traffic
- Weather conditions

## Knowledge is Power
The estimate changed --- not because reality changed,<br>
but because your knowledge improved.

## On the Road
You start driving. As you continue, the ETA keeps changing:

- 2h 21m
- 2h 27m
- 2h 25m

## What's Going On?
Two different things are happening:

- You are learning more as new information comes in
- The world itself is unpredictable

These are fundamentally different sources of uncertainty.

## Thought Experiment
Imagine an all-knowing navigation system. It knows:

- fine-grained driver behavior
- future traffic events
- complete system state

## Is this enough?
It could predict your arrival time exactly.<br>
No uncertainty would remain.

## Knowledge Limits (Epistemic)
In reality, your navigation system does not know everything.<br>
As more relevant information is added, the estimate improves.

This is epistemic uncertainty due to limited knowledge.<br>
More relevant information reduces epistemic uncertainty.

## Residual Variability (Aleatoric)
Even with more and more relevant information, the ETA still fluctuates:

- small events cascade (e.g., slight braking waves)
- human behavior varies moment to moment
- the system is inherently dynamic

This remaining uncertainty is aleatoric uncertainty.<br>
At a certain point, the information simply becomes immeasurable.

## Why this Matters
When making decisions, the distinction is critical:

- If uncertainty is epistemic → collect more relevant data
- If uncertainty is aleatoric → plan for variability

Either leave ealier (robustness)<br>
Or communicate a probable time range (uncertainty quantification)

## Takeaway
When predicting your arrival time:
- Some uncertainty comes from not knowing enough yet
- Some uncertainty remains even if you knew everything practical

##

Modeling is the act of making this distinction explicit.
